metadata.scope: Sample expansions for A1 L1-L5 (demonstration of all 120 lessons pattern)
metadata.notes: Each lesson follows the pattern: glossary-expanded (8-12 items) → examples (5-8) → cultural-context → native-speaker-usage → practice-tiered (3 levels)

items.usageContext: Formal greeting, works in all situations

items.regionalVariant: More common in US than UK
Casual US/Australian, less formal in UK

items.definition: おはよう、おはようございます
items.regionalVariant: Universal, though less common in very casual settings

items.definition: こんにちは（午後の時間帯）
こんばんは、こんばんは
さようなら、失礼します
items.regionalVariant: Universal but less frequent in casual daily use

items.synonym: catch you later, see you soon
items.usageContext: Implies you will see them again
items.regionalVariant: Very common in all varieties of English

items.definition: お大事に、達者でね、元気でね
お元気ですか、調子はどう

items.synonym: how's it going, how are you
items.usageContext: Casual, doesn't always expect detailed answer
items.regionalVariant: Very common in US/Australian casual speech

items: Hello! How are you today? - こんにちは！今日はお元気ですか？
Good morning. I slept well. - おはよう。よく眠れました。
Hi there! What's up? - やあ！最近どう？
Good afternoon. Lovely to see you. - こんにちは。お会いして嬉しいです。
Goodbye! Have a great day. - さようなら！いい一日を。
Bye! Talk to you soon. - またね！じゃあ。
Good evening. How has your day been? - こんばんは。今日はどうでしたか？
Take care! See you next week. - 達者でね！また来週。

sections.title: Greetings Across English-Speaking Cultures
sections.content: Greetings in English-speaking countries are more casual and frequent than in Japanese culture. Americans often greet strangers with 'Hi' or 'What's up?' even on first meetings. British English maintains slightly more formality, using 'How do you do?' in very formal contexts. Australians are extremely casual with greetings, often using 'G'day mate' even to people they don't know well. The time-based greeting system (good morning/afternoon/evening) is used more in formal or professional settings. In casual daily life, 'Hi' or 'Hey' dominates. Failure to greet properly in business contexts is considered rude, while being over-formal in casual settings is awkward. In the US, business culture emphasizes building rapport quickly through casual greetings. In the UK, maintaining a degree of formality even in casual settings is valued. In Australia, breaking down hierarchies with very casual greetings is a sign of friendliness, not disrespect.

sections.title: How Native Speakers Actually Greet
sections.content: Native speakers rarely say the complete 'How are you?' in casual contexts - it becomes 'How are you?' or even just 'How's it going?' They often combine greeting + question: 'Hey, how've you been?' Native speakers from different regions use different defaults: Americans prefer 'Hey/Hi', British prefer 'Hello', Australians 'G'day mate'. Most native speakers do NOT expect a detailed answer to 'How are you?' - 'Good, thanks' or 'Not bad' suffices. Using overly detailed answers to casual greetings marks you as non-native. In North America, making eye contact and smiling during greetings is essential. In the UK, less enthusiastic responses are normal and expected. In Australia, humor and self-deprecation during greetings show friendliness.
commonMistakes: Saying 'What's up?' in a job interview (too casual)
Using Australian slang in formal UK business context
Waiting for 'How are you?' to finish speaking before answering
Over-explaining when someone asks 'How are you?' (they don't want your life story)

basic.question: Greet 5 different people at different times of day (7am, noon, 3pm, 6pm, 10pm) using appropriate time-based expressions
basic.answer: 7am: Good morning! / Noon: Good afternoon! / 3pm: Good afternoon! / 6pm: Good evening! / 10pm: Good evening! (or just 'Hi' in casual contexts)

intermediate.question: Create a dialogue: You meet an old friend at a coffee shop unexpectedly. What do you say? Include: greeting, asking how they've been, and farewell.
intermediate.answer: A: 'Hey Sarah! How have you been?' B: 'Great to see you! I've been good. How about you?' A: 'Can't complain. Let's catch up over coffee!' [after conversation] A: 'It was great seeing you. Take care!' B: 'You too! See you soon!'

advanced.question: Research and explain the cultural differences between American, British, and Australian greetings. When would each be inappropriate? Give specific examples.
advanced.answer: Americans: casual 'Hi/Hey/What's up' everywhere, expect quick response. British: more formal 'Hello' in business, casual in leisure, value reserved distance. Australian: very casual 'G'day' to strangers, humor expected. Inappropriate examples: Saying 'What's up?' in a job interview (too casual). Saying 'G'day mate' to a British executive (cultural confusion). Saying 'Good morning' at 9pm (wrong time zone awareness). Using 'What's up?' with elderly people (age inappropriate).

items.definition: ベッドから起きる、起床する
items.usageContext: Leave bed, usually after waking up

items.regionalVariant: US/AU use 'lunch'. UK traditionally 'dinner' for main meal at lunch
US: main evening meal. UK: can mean lunch (formal) or light evening snack. AU: main evening meal

items.definition: 運動する、エクササイズをする
くつろぐ、リラックスする

items.usageContext: Natural state of rest, night sleep or nap

items.definition: シャワーを浴びる、シャワーをする
items.regionalVariant: UK: 'have a shower'. US: 'take a shower'

items.definition: 日々の習慣、決まった手順

items: I wake up at 7 am every weekday. - 私は平日毎日午前7時に目を覚まします。
After waking up, I shower and have breakfast. - 目を覚めた後、シャワーを浴びて朝食を食べます。
I usually have lunch at 12:30 pm at my office. - 私は通常オフィスで午後12時30分に昼食をとります。
I work from 9 am to 5 pm at a marketing company. - 私はマーケティング会社で午前9時から午後5時まで働きます。
In the evening, I exercise at the gym for one hour. - 夕方、私はジムで1時間運動します。
After work, I relax by watching movies or reading. - 仕事の後、映画を見たり本を読んだりしてくつろぎます。
I usually have dinner around 7 pm with my family. - 私は通常午後7時頃に家族と夕食をとります。
I go to sleep around 11 pm every night. - 私は毎晩約午後11時に寝ます。

sections.title: Daily Routines and Work Culture Across English-Speaking Countries
sections.content: Daily routines and meal times vary significantly across English-speaking countries. In the United States, breakfast is typically 7-8am, lunch is 12-1pm (often taken at a desk), and dinner (the main meal) is 6-8pm. In the United Kingdom, breakfast is similar, but 'lunch' is the midday meal, 'tea' or 'supper' is a light evening meal, and 'dinner' can mean either lunch (formal/traditional) or the main evening meal (modern). Australians use 'lunch' for midday meal and 'dinner' for evening meal. Work culture also differs significantly: Americans often work through lunch and view long hours as a sign of commitment. British workers value work-life balance more and take proper lunch breaks. Australians have strong union traditions protecting break times. Starting work at 9am and ending at 5pm is standard in Western countries, but varies by field, country, and company. In Japan, starting earlier (8am) is common, but this is less typical in English-speaking countries.

sections.title: How Native Speakers Talk About Daily Routines
sections.content: Native speakers typically use simple present tense for routines: 'I get up at 7' (not 'I am getting up' or 'I do get up usually'). They use adverbs of frequency: 'I always/usually/sometimes/never wake up early'. They shorten phrases: 'Have breakfast' not 'Eat breakfast' (though both work). Native speakers from the UK say 'have a shower' while Americans say 'take a shower'. In casual conversation, native speakers often combine activities: 'I shower, get dressed, and have breakfast' using present simple. Time expressions follow natural order: wake up → breakfast → work → lunch → work → exercise → dinner → relax → sleep. Native speakers often omit personal pronouns: 'Woke up late, had to rush to work.'
commonMistakes: Using 'I am waking up at 7' (should be 'I wake up at 7' - present simple for habits)
Saying 'I have breakfast' when you are currently eating (not present continuous)
Using 'eat breakfast' instead of 'have breakfast' (native speakers prefer 'have')
Saying 'I work from 9 to 5 o'clock' (native speakers omit 'o'clock' with numbers)
Over-explaining every detail of your routine (native speakers expect brevity)

basic.question: Write your daily routine in 6 simple sentences using present simple tense
basic.answer: I wake up at 7 am. I have breakfast at 7:30. I go to work at 8:30. I eat lunch at noon. I work until 5 pm. I sleep at 11 pm.

intermediate.question: Describe what you do on a typical weekday vs weekend. What activities change?
intermediate.answer: Weekday: wake at 7, have breakfast, work 9-5, lunch at office, dinner at 7, sleep 11. Weekend: wake at 9, relaxed breakfast, maybe go to gym, spend time with friends, dinner out sometimes, sleep at midnight.

advanced.question: Research and compare daily routines and work culture in three English-speaking countries (US, UK, Australia). Which has the healthiest lifestyle? Which is most stressful? Explain reasons.
advanced.answer: UK: has longer lunch breaks and stronger work-life balance (healthier). US: often works through lunch, longer work hours (more stressful). Australia: strong outdoor culture, union protection of break times (balanced). Reasons: UK values personal time, US values productivity/efficiency, AU values health/nature balance.

implementation_notes.pattern: Each of 120 lessons follows identical structure with level-appropriate content
implementation_notes.vocabulary_count: 8-12 items per lesson (increases complexity at higher levels)
implementation_notes.examples_count: 5-8 examples per lesson with Japanese translations
implementation_notes.cultural_sections: Custom content for each lesson's theme
implementation_notes.total_content_volume: ~2000 new lines of content across 120 lessons
